See Fig. 5 for a visual depiction of this relaxed optimization problem and App. G for the details of the proof. 7 C ONCLUSION In this work, we extend and generalize a long line of literature studying the maximum-margin bias of gradient descent to quasi-homogeneous networks. We show that after reaching a point of sep- arability, the gradient ’¼éow dynamics are driven by a competition between maximizing the margin in function space and minimizing the ╬ømax-seminorm in parameter space. We demonstrate, with a simple linear example, how this strong favoritism for the highest-rate parameters can degrade the ro- bustness of quasi-homogeneous models and conjecture that this process, when possible, will reduce the model to a sparser parameterization. Additionally, by applying our theorem to suf’¼üciently ex- pressive neural networks with normalization layers, we reveal a universal mechanism behind Neural Collapse. Here we propose some future directions for this work. Discretization effect. In this work, we only considered gradient ’¼éow, but generalizing the theo- retical results to (stochastic) gradient descent is an important future step. In particular, it is well understood that the discretization effect introduced by a ’¼ünite learning rate has empirically measur- able effects for parameters that are scale-invariant, such as those before normalization layers. While gradient ’¼éow would predict the norm of these parameters to be constant through training, gradient descent predicts that they monotonically diverge, as demonstrated by Kunin et al. (2020). Thus, extending our results to the setting of gradient descent could reshape Theorem 4.1. Optimality of convergence points. We are only able to guarantee by Theorem 4.1 that the learning dynamics will converge to a ’¼ürst-order KKT point of the constrained optimization problem, but not whether this point is locally or globally optimal. Better understanding the landscape of this opti- mization problem and determining when stronger statements can be made is a promising direction for future work. Works such as Chizat & Bach (2020); Ji & Telgarsky (2020); Vardi et al. (2021); Lyu et al. (2021) have made progress in this direction for simple homogeneous networks and could provide a strategy for investigating more complex quasi-homogeneous models. In’¼éuence of initialization. A major limitation of analyzing the maximum-margin bias of gradient ’¼éow is that the dynamics in this terminal phase of training are slow to converge or only become evident at extremely unpractical training loss levels. Motivated by this limitation, Woodworth et al. (2020) and Moroshko et al. (2020) studied the gradient ’¼éow trajectories for diagonal linear networks and showed that there is a transition from a ŌĆ£kernelŌĆØ regime to a ŌĆ£richŌĆØ regime controlled by the scale of the initialization and the ’¼ünal training loss level. Extending this analysis to quasi-homogeneous networks would be a valuable future direction. Impact on performance. An important takeaway from our work is that the maximum-margin bias can actually degrade the performance of a quasi-homogeneous model. The bene’¼üt depends on the parameterization of a model and its relationship to the geometry of the data. Better understanding this interaction could be essential for diagnosing performance gaps of modern neural networks and provide a route towards designing robust architectures. 9Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2023 ACKNOWLEDGMENTS We thank Kaifeng Lyu, Ben Sorscher, Daniel Soudry, and Hidenori Tanaka for helpful discussions. While all ensembles of positive-homogeneous models are quasi-homogeneous, not all quasi- homogeneous models are ensembles. Here we provide a short list of quasi-homogeneous models that cannot be written in the form of Eq. 13. Deep fully connected network with biases. All fully connected networks with biases, and a depth greater than two, are quasi-homogeneous models, but not an ensemble of positive-homogeneous models. Networks with degenerate ╬ø. (2019a) for ensembles of positive-homogeneous models, but also apply to a far more general class of non-homogeneous models. 16Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2023 B T HE CONSISTENCY OF THEOREM 4.1 AND THE PROPER CHOICE OF ╬ø As brie’¼éy discussed in section 4, for a quasi-homogeneous function f, the value of ╬ø, and the ╬╗max parameter set, is not necessarily unique and therefore one may think Theorem 4.1 looks inconsistent. A complete proof of uniqueness of the resulting optimization problem As we discussed above going through three examples, we can identify which╬╗ we should choose, or which╬╗max parameters we should choose, solely by analyzing the model, independent of the data set. In this section, we generalize the previous discussions on examples and prove that the resulting ’¼ürst-order KKT condition derived from Theorem 4.1 is unique, even if the model itself is quasi-homogeneous with multiple choices of scaling parameters ╬ø. While, many modern neural network architectures are ╬ø-quasi-homogeneous, this class of models cannot represent models with non-homogeneous activations such as the hyperbolic tangent function or the sigmoid function. While these activation functions are less common in practice, it would be interesting if there exists a direction towards generalizing our analysis to models us- ing these functions. This could be of interest to the computational biology community as these monotonic activations that saturate are much more biologically-plausible then non-saturating homo- geneous activations. One route could be studying the properties of homothetic functions, which are monotonic transformations of homogeneous functions. This class of functions has the same ordinal properties of homogeneous functions and is used extensively in economics Simon et al. (1994). A2 - A5. These assumptions are equivalent, up to order, to the assumptions presented in Lyu & Li (2019) and are all quite standard in the literature studying the maximum margin bias of gradient descent. The strongest of these assumptions is A4, which assumes the training dynamics of the model are governed by the ’¼ürst-order ODE gradient ’¼éow. As discussed in section 7, an important future step would be to generalize our results to stochastic gradient descent (SGD). It is very possible that the hyperparameters of SGD, such as the learning rate and batch size, play an important role in determining the forces driving the limiting dynamics. A6. This assumption implies the convergence of the decision boundary and is equivalent to direc- tional convergence for a homogeneous model. This assumption is necessary to show that the modelŌĆÖs prediction is bounded on the normalized training trajectory (Lemma E.1) and for a technical reason to show the alignment of d╬Ė dt and ╬ø╬Ė (Lemma E.4). While a necessary assumption, we expect that this assumption can be weakened by exploiting the argument in Ji & Telgarsky (2020), which was applied for homogeneous functions. This could be an important step for future work as it is possible to construct settings where gradient ’¼éow will violate this assumption. See Appendix J of Lyu & Li (2019) for an example of a smooth homogeneous function where the limiting dynamics of gradient ’¼éow provably donŌĆÖt converge after normalization, but move along a circle. Another, more practical example, occurs for models where the residual block diverges. Because these parameters necessarily have a ╬╗ value of 0, then the normalized parameter ╦å╬Ė diverge as well. The divergence of a residual block essentially means that the skip connection of the model is effectively negligible and does not play an important role in the classi’¼ücation. Thus, we believe that if the skip connections play an im- portant role for the classi’¼ücation task, then residual block does not diverge, and this assumption is satis’¼üed. Additionally, like A7, this assumption restricts the possible proper values of╬ø as discussed in App. B. A7. This assumption implies that ╬╗max parameters play a role in the classi’¼ücation task. This is the strongest of the assumptions we introduce in our work. This assumption is a restriction on the interaction of the parameterization of a model and the dataset, and thus it is dif’¼ücult to assert its validity for general settings, without directly modeling the data as in section 5. That said, there are some standard settings where we can assert that this assumption is always true. First, it is trivially true that for a homogeneous model whereŌłź╦å╬ĖŌłź╬ømax =Ōłź╦å╬ĖŌłź that A7 is always true. Additionally, for all models with batch normalization on the last hidden layer, such as a ResNet-18 model, then A7 is also true, as the last layer parameters are ╬ømax parameters, and thus necessary for classi’¼ücation. However, there are other settings where it is less clear that A7 is satis’¼üed. For example, for a fully- connected network with bias parameters the validity of A7 will depend on the data. This limitation is why we introduced Conjecture 5.1, which does not involve A7, and provided empirical evidence supporting its claim in section 5. The challenge to proving Conjecture 5.1 will be showing that a version of Lemma 4.1 still holds once the parameter space is restricted such that the╬ømax parameters are zero. It is likely the case that an argument in this direction will require a proof by induction on the highest-rate parameters and their importance to the classi’¼ücation task. 24Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2023 D P ROOF OF LEMMA 4.1 Throughout this section, we assume A1 to A4. llm_extracted_info.main_contributions: Studies implicit maximum-margin bias of gradient flow on exponential-tail losses (e.g., cross-entropy) for a broad class of non-homogeneous neural nets. Introduces ╬ø-quasi-homogeneous models (covering most modern feedforward nets with homogeneous activations, including biases, residual connections, pooling/normalization). Proves that after reaching separability, gradient flow drives parameters (up to scaling along characteristic curves) to first-order KKT points of a maximum-margin problem that minimizes an asymmetric seminorm involving only highest-rate (╬╗_max) parameters: minimize 1/2||╬Ė||^2_{╬ø_max} s.t. y_i f(x_i;╬Ė)Ōēź1. Shows this asymmetry can harm robustness via an analytic ball-separation example where robustness collapses as data geometry makes highest-rate parameters optional. Conjectures a stronger ŌĆ£cascading minimizationŌĆØ behavior where, if ╬╗_max parameters are unnecessary for separability, training zeros them and proceeds to the next rate class (lexicographic sparsification). Applies theory to sufficiently expressive normalized networks and derives an optimization whose global optima exhibit Neural Collapse, providing a universal mechanism linking parameter-space bias to Neural Collapse observations.
llm_extracted_info.methodology: Defines ╬ø-quasi-homogeneity via parameter scaling Žł_╬▒(╬Ė)=exp(╬▒╬ø)╬Ė such that f(x;Žł_╬▒(╬Ė))=e^╬▒ f(x;╬Ė) (╬ø diagonal WLOG). Develops geometric analysis using characteristic curves generated by ╬ø╬Ė, ╬ø-seminorm ||╬Ė||_╬ø, and ╬ø-normalized parameters \hat{╬Ė} on unit ╬ø-ellipsoid. Analyzes gradient flow dynamics on exponential loss using Clarke subdifferentials for nonsmooth nets, proving inequalities for growth of ||╬Ė||_╬ø and monotonicity/convergence of a smooth normalized margin \tilde{╬│} = log((nL)^{-1}) / ||╬Ė||^{╬╗_max^{-1}}_╬ø. Shows ||╬Ė||_╬øŌåÆŌł× and alignment between d╬Ė/dt and ╬ø╬Ė (angle ŽēŌåÆ0 on a subsequence), implying convergence along a characteristic curve and yielding first-order KKT conditions for the asymmetric max-margin problem. Extends to multiclass cross-entropy (appendix) with modified separability threshold. For robustness section, solves resulting convex programs analytically for two disjoint balls under different parameterizations. For Neural Collapse, reduces quasi-homogeneous max-margin objective in normalized-feature networks to a constrained optimization over last-layer weights/biases/features; proves global optima form a centered regular simplex and nearest-class-mean decision rule.
llm_extracted_info.experimental_setup: Primarily theoretical with supporting synthetic experiments. Fig.1: binary logistic regression on 2D data sampled from two Gaussians N(┬▒╬╝,ŽāI) (100 points total, Žā=0.25), trained with full-batch gradient descent (╬Ę=0.5, 1e5 steps) under two parameterizations; compares learned separator to maximum Ōäō2-margin solution computed via scikit-learn SVM. Fig.4: R^3 ball-separation task with 1e4 points sampled from surfaces of balls B(┬▒╬╝,r) across 100 radii rŌłł[0,1]; quasi-homogeneous diagonal product parameterization with heterogeneous depths D1=1, D2=5, D3=10 giving ╬ø=[1,0.2,0.1]; initializes ╬Ė=1 vector; simulates gradient flow using SciPy ODE IVP solver up to T=1e5; tracks effective linear classifier components and robustness l(w) vs radius; compares to homogeneous model and theoretical predictions (Lemma 5.1) and conjectured cascading behavior. Neural Collapse results are theoretical (no new large-scale benchmarks), relying on assumptions of layer normalization and sufficient expressivity.
llm_extracted_info.limitations: Relies on gradient flow (continuous-time) analysis; finite-step (stochastic) gradient descent and discretization/learning-rate effects can change behavior, especially for scale-invariant parameters before normalization. Requires several assumptions: quasi-homogeneity with some ╬ø; regularity/local Lipschitz and chain rule; strong separability after some time; convergence of ╬ø-normalized parameters (A6) which may fail in some architectures/trajectories; and a key conditional separability assumption (A7) ensuring ╬╗_max parameters are necessaryŌĆöotherwise theorem doesnŌĆÖt apply (motivating Conjecture 5.1). Guarantees only convergence to first-order KKT points, not global optima; landscape may include saddles/local minima so Neural Collapse is proven for global solutions of the derived optimization, not necessarily for all training runs. Neural Collapse mechanism assumes normalization (e.g., layer norm) and strong expressivity (A8) that may not hold in practice; does not address non-homogeneous activations (tanh/sigmoid) or other losses without exponential tails beyond discussed extensions.
llm_extracted_info.future_research_directions: Extend theory from gradient flow to (stochastic) gradient descent, quantifying discretization-induced implicit regularization and effects on scale-invariant/╬╗=0 parameters (especially with normalization). Prove or refine the Cascading Minimization conjecture (remove A7) via inductive/lexicographic analysis and characterize when higher-rate parameters are discarded. Strengthen convergence results by weakening A6 using nonsmooth KL/trajectory alignment techniques (e.g., Ji & Telgarsky-style arguments). Analyze optimality: characterize when KKT limit points are globally optimal; study landscape/strict saddle properties for quasi-homogeneous max-margin problems. Investigate dependence on initialization scale and finite loss levels (kernel-to-rich regime transitions) for quasi-homogeneous nets. Study practical impacts: how asymmetric norm bias interacts with data geometry and robustness, and how architecture/parameterization choices (biases, residual paths, normalization placement) can be designed/regularized to mitigate robustness degradation or control sparsification/Neural Collapse. Explore generalizations to broader function classes (e.g., homothetic functions) and to non-homogeneous activations.

research_hypothesis.open_problems: Prompt-only Chain-of-Thought (CoT) improvements often increase apparent ŌĆ£reasoningŌĆØ without improving faithfulness: models can produce internally inconsistent steps yet a correct-looking final answer, and self-verification/critique can collude when the same model judges itself. Existing prompt-only fixes are typically evaluated only on final-answer accuracy, which fails to penalize (i) contradiction between intermediate claims and the final answer, (ii) misuse/omission of question constraints, and (iii) overconfident wrong answers under a fixed token budget. The key gap is a prompt-only mechanism that *binds* the final answer to a compact, auditable set of intermediate commitments and provides a token-budgeted reliability gain even when the ŌĆ£verifierŌĆØ is not an independent model.
research_hypothesis.method:
Propose **Commitment-Checked Chain-of-Thought (C3oT)**: a prompt-only, two-pass routine that replaces free-form CoT with a human-like ŌĆ£commit ŌåÆ compute ŌåÆ reconcileŌĆØ workflow.

Core novelty: instead of asking the model to ŌĆśverify its reasoning,ŌĆÖ C3oT forces the model to emit a small set of *structured commitments* (a scratchpad contract) that can be mechanically checked in Python (and partially checked by the model). This reduces verifier collusion because the acceptance criteria are external.

Procedure (single sample, fixed budget):
1) **Commit pass (C-pass)**: The model outputs a JSON with: (a) variables and units, (b) extracted constraints from the question, (c) a plan of 2ŌĆō4 steps, and crucially (d) a set of 1ŌĆō3 *checkable invariants* (e.g., equations, inequalities, conservation/total sums, parity, monotonicity) that must hold for the final answer. No full solution yet.
2) **Solve pass (S-pass)**: The model solves concisely and outputs FINAL plus a minimal ŌĆ£evidence tableŌĆØ mapping each invariant to the numeric substitution.
3) **External checker + targeted repair**: A Python script validates (i) JSON schema, (ii) invariant satisfaction, (iii) constraint coverage (string/slot match), and (iv) numeric consistency between FINAL and evidence table. If any check fails, the model gets one short repair prompt that includes the failing invariant and is required to *change either the invariant or the answer* explicitly (diff-style), preventing silent drift.

Why novel vs prior critique-and-revise: the key mechanism is **prompt-induced structured commitments + programmatic invariant checking** as the objective surrogate, giving a measurable faithfulness target under prompt-only constraints. It reframes token-budgeted reliability as satisfying externally checkable contracts, not self-rated confidence.
research_hypothesis.experimental_setup:
Datasets (small, scriptable):
- GSM8K (math word problems), 200-item subset.
- SVAMP, 200-item subset.
Optionally add: MultiArith 200-item subset for diversity.

Baselines (prompt-only):
A) Direct Answer (no CoT)
B) Standard CoT (bounded length)
C) Self-Refine / Critique-then-Revise (single critique loop)
D) Self-Consistency (k=5) if budget allows

Proposed:
- C3oT with (i) commitment JSON, (ii) invariants, (iii) external checker, (iv) Ōēż1 repair.

Metrics recorded:
- Primary: exact-match accuracy of final answer.
- Secondary: (1) invariant-pass rate after first attempt, (2) post-repair pass rate, (3) tokens/cost, (4) ŌĆ£unfaithful-correctŌĆØ rate = correct FINAL but invariant check fails (should decrease), (5) refusal/parse-failure rate.

Design notes for feasibility:
- Invariants are limited to simple arithmetic/equality/inequality statements over extracted variables; checker uses sympy-safe eval or numeric eval with tolerance.
- If the model cannot produce good invariants, it will show up as low invariant-pass and no accuracy gain, making hypothesis falsifiable with a Python loop.
research_hypothesis.expected_result:
On GSM8K-200 under a fixed max-token budget (e.g., 350 tokens total across passes), expect:
- Standard CoT accuracy: ~0.72
- Critique-then-Revise (single loop) accuracy: ~0.74
- C3oT accuracy: ~0.77ŌĆō0.79 (Ōēł +0.05 to +0.07 over CoT)
- Self-consistency k=5: ~0.79ŌĆō0.81 but at ~4ŌĆō5├Ś token cost.

Secondary expectations:
- ŌĆ£Unfaithful-correctŌĆØ (correct final but invariant failure) should drop by Ōēź50% relative to standard CoT.
- First-pass invariant-pass rate ~55ŌĆō65%, post-repair pass rate ~75ŌĆō85%.
- Average token usage: ~1.4ŌĆō1.8├Ś standard CoT, still far below self-consistency.
research_hypothesis.expected_conclusion: Structured, externally checkable commitments (invariants) make prompt-only CoT more *reliable per token* by binding the final answer to a small set of auditable constraints, reducing both silent reasoning drift and overconfident wrong answers without fine-tuning. Academically, the contribution is a new prompt-only objective surrogateŌĆö*contract satisfaction*ŌĆöand an evaluation lens that separates final-answer accuracy from faithfulness (unfaithful-correct). Socially, it offers a low-latency, low-cost way to improve trustworthiness in constrained deployments (education, customer support, triage) where verbose but inconsistent reasoning is harmful.

experimental_design.experiment_summary:
Goal: demonstrate that Commitment-Checked Chain-of-Thought (C3oT) improves *reliability per token* for grade-school math word problems by binding the final numeric answer to a compact set of externally checkable commitments.

Task the model performs: For each math word problem, the model must produce a single numeric answer (and, depending on method, intermediate structured artifacts) under a fixed token budget. We evaluate not only whether the final number is correct, but whether intermediate commitments (constraints/invariants) are internally consistent with the final output.

Workflow (per example, fixed budget):
1) Proposed C3oT runs a two-pass inference routine:
   - C-pass: model outputs a *commitment JSON* containing (i) variables/units (nullable), (ii) extracted constraints, (iii) 2ŌĆō4 step plan, (iv) 1ŌĆō3 checkable invariants expressed over variables and FINAL.
   - S-pass: model outputs a concise solution with `FINAL: <number>` and an evidence line tying invariants to the FINAL.
2) External checker (Python) validates: JSON schema, parseability, invariant satisfiability against the produced FINAL (and any bound variables), and basic constraint-coverage heuristics.
3) If any check fails, one *targeted repair* prompt is allowed. The model must explicitly change either invariants or FINAL (no silent drift).

Baseline method: Standard bounded Chain-of-Thought (CoT) producing a final numeric answer under the same token budget.

Scale fitted to Runner (4 vCPU/16GB):
- Use the GSM8K test split with a deterministic 200-item subset (seeded sampling) to keep runtime and API cost bounded.
- No training/fine-tuning; inference-only prompting + lightweight Python checking.
- Single model, single dataset, and at most one repair round per example to cap calls (C3oT uses up to 3 calls/example; baseline uses 1 call/example). Parallelism can be limited (e.g., 4 workers) to match CPU/RAM and avoid rate-limit bursts.

evaluation_metrics.name: accuracy
evaluation_metrics.description:
Correctness criteria: a prediction is correct if the extracted numeric value after the token `FINAL:` matches the gold answer numerically within tolerance.

Calculation method:
1) Extract prediction: parse `FINAL:\s*([-+]?\d+(?:\.\d+)?)`; if missing, fall back to last number in output. Convert to float.
2) Extract gold: parse the GSM8K gold answer (commonly contains a final number at the end); extract last number and convert to float.
3) Compare with relative tolerance: `|pred-gold| <= tol * max(1, |pred|, |gold|)` with `tol=1e-9` (or `1e-6` if formatting noise occurs).
4) `accuracy = (# correct) / N`.

Task appropriateness: GSM8K is numeric-answer generation; numeric tolerance avoids penalizing harmless formatting differences.

Relevant visualizations:
- Bar chart of accuracy by method.
- Bootstrap 95% CI error bars for accuracy (resampling over questions).

evaluation_metrics.name: invariant_first_pass_rate
evaluation_metrics.description:
Correctness criteria: for C3oT only, an example counts as passing if (i) the commitment JSON parses and meets schema, and (ii) all listed invariants evaluate to True when executed in a restricted Python environment using available variable bindings plus `FINAL` extracted from the S-pass.

Calculation method:
- For each example, run checker after the first S-pass.
- `rate = (# examples with checker_ok) / N`.

Task appropriateness: measures whether the model can produce *auditable, checkable* intermediate commitments that actually constrain the final answer, directly targeting faithfulness rather than just outcome.

Relevant visualizations:
- Stacked bar: pass vs fail proportions.
- Histogram of number of invariants produced vs pass rate (binned).

evaluation_metrics.name: invariant_post_repair_rate
evaluation_metrics.description:
Correctness criteria: for C3oT only, an example counts as passing if it passes the checker after allowing up to one targeted repair round.

Calculation method:
- If first-pass fails, issue one repair prompt containing the failing check note.
- Re-run checker on updated commitments + revised solution.
- `rate = (# examples passing after repair) / N`.

Task appropriateness: quantifies whether targeted, externally grounded feedback can recover faithfulness without multiple free-form self-critiques.

Relevant visualizations:
- Sankey/flow diagram: first-pass pass/fail ŌåÆ after-repair pass/fail.
- Bar chart comparing first-pass vs post-repair rates.

evaluation_metrics.name: unfaithful_correct_rate
evaluation_metrics.description:
Correctness criteria: an example is counted as *unfaithful-correct* if the final answer is correct by the `accuracy` criterion but the invariant checker fails (after the first pass; optionally also report after repair).

Calculation method:
- For each example, compute `correct` (numeric match) and `checker_ok`.
- Indicator: `unfaithful_correct = 1 if correct==1 and checker_ok==0 else 0`.
- `rate = sum(unfaithful_correct)/N`.

Task appropriateness: directly addresses the hypothesis that prompt-only CoT can be correct for the wrong reasons; this metric penalizes internal inconsistency even when the final number matches.

Relevant visualizations:
- Side-by-side bars: correct&faithful vs correct&unfaithful vs incorrect.
- Scatter plot: confidence proxy (e.g., presence of assertive language) vs unfaithful-correct (optional if extracted).

evaluation_metrics.name: parse_failure_rate
evaluation_metrics.description:
Correctness criteria: for C3oT only, a parse failure occurs if the C-pass output is not valid JSON or lacks required keys (`variables`, `constraints`, `plan`, `invariants`).

Calculation method:
- Attempt `json.loads` on C-pass.
- Validate minimal schema.
- `rate = (# failures)/N`.

Task appropriateness: C3oT relies on structured commitments; robustness to formatting is essential for deployability.

Relevant visualizations:
- Bar chart of failure rate.
- Table of top failure modes (missing key, invalid JSON, empty invariants).

evaluation_metrics.name: token_efficiency
evaluation_metrics.description:
Correctness criteria: measures accuracy achieved per generated token under a fixed budget.

Calculation method:
- Record generated tokens per API call (if provider returns) or approximate via a tokenizer (e.g., tiktoken-like approximation; for Gemini, use returned usage metadata if available).
- Compute: `token_efficiency = accuracy / (avg_generated_tokens_per_example)`; report also raw avg tokens.

Task appropriateness: the hypothesis emphasizes token-budgeted reliability; efficiency captures improvements that do not simply spend more tokens.

Relevant visualizations:
- Scatter plot: accuracy vs avg tokens (Pareto frontier).
- Bar chart: avg tokens by method.

proposed_method.description: Inference-only, two-pass prompting that replaces free-form CoT with structured commitments (variables, constraints, plan, and 1ŌĆō3 checkable invariants). A Python checker externally validates invariant satisfaction and schema compliance; if failing, one targeted repair prompt forces an explicit change to either invariants or FINAL, reducing self-verifier collusion and binding the final answer to auditable contracts under a fixed token budget.

comparative_methods.description: Single-pass chain-of-thought prompting with a strict instruction to keep reasoning brief and end with `FINAL: <number>`. No external checking, no structured commitments, no repair loop; matched to the same overall token budget to test whether C3oT improves reliability beyond merely generating more text.
